title: Preprocessor and macros

canonicalExplanation.summary: The preprocessor performs token-based inclusion, conditional compilation, and macro replacement before C translation, without type checking or ordinary function evaluation.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A function-like SQUARE macro is rejected for i++ because multiple argument substitution would modify i more than once. Your goal is to replace unsafe macro patterns and explain preprocessing results. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Failure-safe cleanup; Scope, linkage and storage duration; Header interfaces and include guards; Preprocess, compile, assemble and link. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: The preprocessor performs token-based inclusion, conditional compilation, and macro replacement before C translation, without type checking or ordinary function evaluation. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Macro arguments can be evaluated multiple times, precedence can change without parentheses, names can collide, and conditional paths can rot when rarely built. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: A function-like macro behaves exactly like an inline function and evaluates each argument once.

includes: Use conditional compilation and simple macros while avoiding multiple evaluation and hidden control flow.
Replace unsafe macro patterns and explain preprocessing results.
excludes: Macro arguments can be evaluated multiple times, precedence can change without parentheses, names can collide, and conditional paths can rot when rarely built.

outcomes: Replace unsafe macro patterns and explain preprocessing results.

examples.title: Preprocessor and macros: defined case
examples.situation: A function-like SQUARE macro is rejected for i++ because multiple argument substitution would modify i more than once.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A function-like SQUARE macro is rejected for i++ because multiple argument substitution would modify i more than once.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Macros are token replacement; parenthesize carefully, avoid side-effecting arguments, and prefer typed functions or constants when possible.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Macros are token replacement; parenthesize carefully, avoid side-effecting arguments, and prefer typed functions or constants when possible.

examples.title: Preprocessor and macros: boundary case
examples.situation: A small constant uses an enum or const object when type and scope matter instead of a global textual macro.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A small constant uses an enum or const object when type and scope matter instead of a global textual macro.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Macro arguments can be evaluated multiple times, precedence can change without parentheses, names can collide, and conditional paths can rot when rarely built.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: A function-like macro behaves exactly like an inline function and evaluates each argument once.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Replace unsafe macro patterns and explain preprocessing results.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A function-like macro behaves exactly like an inline function and evaluates each argument once.
misconceptions.correction: Macros are token replacement; parenthesize carefully, avoid side-effecting arguments, and prefer typed functions or constants when possible.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: A function-like macro behaves exactly like an inline function and evaluates each argument once.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Preprocessor and macros: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Macro arguments can be evaluated multiple times, precedence can change without parentheses, names can collide, and conditional paths can rot when rarely built.
